data.title: LINK Mobility Group Holding ASA Just Missed Earnings - But Analysts Have Updated Their Models
data.description:
It's been a mediocre week for LINK Mobility Group Holding ASA (OB:LINK) shareholders, with the stock dropping 16% to kr21.90 in the week since its latest yearly results. It looks like a pretty bad result, all things considered. Although revenues of kr7.1b were in line with analyst predictions, statutory earnings fell badly short, missing estimates by 38% to hit kr0.28 per share. The analysts typically update their forecasts at each earnings report, and we can judge from their estimates whether their view of the company has changed or if there are any new concerns to be aware of. We've gathered the most recent statutory forecasts to see whether the analysts have changed their earnings models, following these results.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Taking into account the latest results, the current consensus from LINK Mobility Group Holding's four analysts is for revenues of kr8.35b in 2026. This would reflect a meaningful 18% increase on its revenue over the past 12 months. Statutory earnings per share are predicted to soar 247% to kr1.04. Before this earnings report, the analysts had been forecasting revenues of kr9.04b and earnings per share (EPS) of kr1.60 in 2026. From this we can that sentiment has definitely become more bearish after the latest results, leading to lower revenue forecasts and a pretty serious reduction to earnings per share estimates.

View our latest analysis for LINK Mobility Group Holding

It'll come as no surprise then, to learn that the analysts have cut their price target 23% to kr33.50. There's another way to think about price targets though, and that's to look at the range of price targets put forward by analysts, because a wide range of estimates could suggest a diverse view on possible outcomes for the business. The most optimistic LINK Mobility Group Holding analyst has a price target of kr43.00 per share, while the most pessimistic values it at kr21.00. This is a fairly broad spread of estimates, suggesting that analysts are forecasting a wide range of possible outcomes for the business.

Another way we can view these estimates is in the context of the bigger picture, such as how the forecasts stack up against past performance, and whether forecasts are more or less bullish relative to other companies in the industry. The analysts are definitely expecting LINK Mobility Group Holding's growth to accelerate, with the forecast 18% annualised growth to the end of 2026 ranking favourably alongside historical growth of 14% per annum over the past five years. By contrast, our data suggests that other companies (with analyst coverage) in a similar industry are forecast to grow their revenue at 9.5% per year. Factoring in the forecast acceleration in revenue, it's pretty clear that LINK Mobility Group Holding is expected to grow much faster than its industry.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The biggest concern is that the analysts reduced their earnings per share estimates, suggesting business headwinds could lay ahead for LINK Mobility Group Holding. They also downgraded LINK Mobility Group Holding's revenue estimates, but industry data suggests that it is expected to grow faster than the wider industry. The consensus price target fell measurably, with the analysts seemingly not reassured by the latest results, leading to a lower estimate of LINK Mobility Group Holding's future valuation.

Keeping that in mind, we still think that the longer term trajectory of the business is much more important for investors to consider. We have forecasts for LINK Mobility Group Holding going out to 2028, and you can see them free on our platform here.

That said, it's still necessary to consider the ever-present spectre of investment risk. We've identified 3 warning signs with LINK Mobility Group Holding , and understanding them should be part of your investment process.

data.title: Is Atos (ENXTPA:ATO) Pricing In A Turnaround After Major Restructuring Efforts And Volatile Returns
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Atos at €44.50 is priced for a turnaround or still carrying too much baggage, you are not alone. This article focuses squarely on what the current share price implies about value.
  • The stock has been volatile, with a 7.5% decline over the last 7 days and a 21.7% decline over the past month. It still shows a 64.8% return over the last year after very large losses over 3 and 5 years, at 95.7% and 99.0% respectively.
  • Recent headlines around Atos have mainly centered on its restructuring efforts and business refocusing, as the company works to reshape its operations and simplify its structure. Investors have been watching these developments closely as they assess what the revamped group could be worth and how the balance of risks is shifting.
  • On our checks, Atos scores a 5/6 valuation rating. We will unpack this using multiple methods before circling back at the end of the article to show an even more practical way to think about what that valuation really means for you.

Atos delivered 64.8% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the IT industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Atos Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes projected future cash flows, then discounts them back to today using a required rate of return, to estimate what the whole business might be worth right now.

For Atos, the latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of €213.38m. Analyst and model projections used here show free cash flow moving to €106m by 2028, with further estimates extending out to 2035 based on a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. All cash flows in this model are in €.

Aggregating and discounting these projected cash flows produces an estimated intrinsic value of about €213.33 per share. Compared with a current share price of €44.50, the model output implies the shares trade at a 79.1% discount to this estimate. On this DCF view, the stock screens as materially undervalued given the assumptions used.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Atos is undervalued by 79.1%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 229 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Atos.

Approach 2: Atos Price vs Earnings

P/E is often the go to metric for profitable companies because it links what you pay directly to the earnings the business is generating today. It also tends to capture, in a single number, how optimistic or cautious the market is about those earnings.

In general, higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk usually support a higher P/E, while slower expected growth and higher risk tend to justify a lower P/E. That is why simply comparing P/E ratios across companies only gets you part of the way.

Atos currently trades on a P/E of 0.58x, compared with an IT industry average of 19.22x and a peer average of 16.21x. Simply Wall St also calculates a “Fair Ratio” for Atos of 5.69x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/E could be for the company, based on factors such as its earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and key risks.

Because the Fair Ratio is tailored to Atos, it can be more informative than just lining the stock up against broad industry or peer averages. With the current P/E of 0.58x sitting well below the 5.69x Fair Ratio, this framework points to the shares appearing undervalued on an earnings basis.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Atos Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. These are simple stories you create about Atos that tie your view of its future revenue, earnings, margins and fair value to the current share price. They are easy to track and compare on Simply Wall St's Community page, update automatically as new earnings or news arrive, and can differ meaningfully from other investors. For example, one investor might anchor on a fair value close to €20.60 while another leans toward €55.00. This helps each person see whether they think Atos at €44.50 is above or below their own fair value and therefore whether it looks more like a potential buying or selling opportunity to them.

data.title: Returns On Capital Are Showing Encouraging Signs At Montana Aerospace (VTX:AERO)
data.description:
If you're looking for a multi-bagger, there's a few things to keep an eye out for. Firstly, we'll want to see a proven return on capital employed (ROCE) that is increasing, and secondly, an expanding base of capital employed. This shows us that it's a compounding machine, able to continually reinvest its earnings back into the business and generate higher returns. So when we looked at Montana Aerospace (VTX:AERO) and its trend of ROCE, we really liked what we saw.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Understanding Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)

If you haven't worked with ROCE before, it measures the 'return' (pre-tax profit) a company generates from capital employed in its business. Analysts use this formula to calculate it for Montana Aerospace:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.076 = €106m ÷ (€1.6b - €212m) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

Therefore, Montana Aerospace has an ROCE of 7.6%. In absolute terms, that's a low return and it also under-performs the Aerospace & Defense industry average of 12%.

View our latest analysis for Montana Aerospace

Above you can see how the current ROCE for Montana Aerospace compares to its prior returns on capital, but there's only so much you can tell from the past. If you're interested, you can view the analysts predictions in our free analyst report for Montana Aerospace .

What Can We Tell From Montana Aerospace's ROCE Trend?

We're delighted to see that Montana Aerospace is reaping rewards from its investments and is now generating some pre-tax profits. About five years ago the company was generating losses but things have turned around because it's now earning 7.6% on its capital. Not only that, but the company is utilizing 54% more capital than before, but that's to be expected from a company trying to break into profitability. This can indicate that there's plenty of opportunities to invest capital internally and at ever higher rates, both common traits of a multi-bagger.

On a related note, the company's ratio of current liabilities to total assets has decreased to 13%, which basically reduces it's funding from the likes of short-term creditors or suppliers. So this improvement in ROCE has come from the business' underlying economics, which is great to see.

What We Can Learn From Montana Aerospace's ROCE

Long story short, we're delighted to see that Montana Aerospace's reinvestment activities have paid off and the company is now profitable. And with the stock having performed exceptionally well over the last three years, these patterns are being accounted for by investors. So given the stock has proven it has promising trends, it's worth researching the company further to see if these trends are likely to persist.

Like most companies, Montana Aerospace does come with some risks, and we've found 2 warning signs that you should be aware of.

data.title: Investors Should Be Encouraged By Bharat Electronics' (NSE:BEL) Returns On Capital
data.description:
To find a multi-bagger stock, what are the underlying trends we should look for in a business? One common approach is to try and find a company with returns on capital employed (ROCE) that are increasing, in conjunction with a growing amount of capital employed. This shows us that it's a compounding machine, able to continually reinvest its earnings back into the business and generate higher returns. So when we looked at the ROCE trend of Bharat Electronics (NSE:BEL) we really liked what we saw.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Understanding Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. The formula for this calculation on Bharat Electronics is:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.32 = ₹74b ÷ (₹429b - ₹202b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to December 2025).

Thus, Bharat Electronics has an ROCE of 32%. That's a fantastic return and not only that, it outpaces the average of 17% earned by companies in a similar industry.

See our latest analysis for Bharat Electronics

Above you can see how the current ROCE for Bharat Electronics compares to its prior returns on capital, but there's only so much you can tell from the past. If you're interested, you can view the analysts predictions in our free analyst report for Bharat Electronics .

What Can We Tell From Bharat Electronics' ROCE Trend?

Bharat Electronics is displaying some positive trends. Over the last five years, returns on capital employed have risen substantially to 32%. The company is effectively making more money per dollar of capital used, and it's worth noting that the amount of capital has increased too, by 95%. So we're very much inspired by what we're seeing at Bharat Electronics thanks to its ability to profitably reinvest capital.

Another thing to note, Bharat Electronics has a high ratio of current liabilities to total assets of 47%. This effectively means that suppliers (or short-term creditors) are funding a large portion of the business, so just be aware that this can introduce some elements of risk. Ideally we'd like to see this reduce as that would mean fewer obligations bearing risks.

The Bottom Line On Bharat Electronics' ROCE

To sum it up, Bharat Electronics has proven it can reinvest in the business and generate higher returns on that capital employed, which is terrific. Since the stock has returned a staggering 938% to shareholders over the last five years, it looks like investors are recognizing these changes. Therefore, we think it would be worth your time to check if these trends are going to continue.

Bharat Electronics does have some risks though, and we've spotted 1 warning sign for Bharat Electronics that you might be interested in.

data.title: Teikoku Sen I (TSE:3302) Margin Improvement Reinforces Bullish Narratives Despite Volatile EPS
data.description:
Teikoku Sen-i (TSE:3302) has reported its FY 2025 quarterly results with Q3 revenue of ¥5.8b and basic EPS of ¥17.41, alongside a trailing twelve month EPS of ¥148.84 that reflects earnings growth of 38% over the past year and an improvement in net profit margin to 11.5% from 9.4%. The company has seen quarterly revenue range from ¥3.2b in Q2 FY 2025 to ¥15.3b in Q1 FY 2025, with basic EPS moving from a loss of ¥3.00 in Q2 FY 2025 to ¥93.02 in Q1 FY 2025 and ¥40.76 in Q4 FY 2024. 38% earnings growth but 1% five year drag

  • Over the last 12 months, EPS of ¥148.84 is up 38% year on year, while over five years earnings have declined about 1% per year on average, so you are seeing a strong recent lift sitting against a softer multi year pattern.
  • What stands out for a cautious, bearish view is that the recent EPS lift sits on top of quarterly swings, including a loss of ¥3.00 per share in Q2 FY 2025 and net income moving from a loss of ¥77 million in that quarter to ¥447 million in Q3 FY 2025. This keeps the focus on how steady those higher earnings really are.
    • Bears point to the 1% annual earnings decline over five years as evidence that the 38% trailing improvement may not reflect a simple straight line trend.
    • The move from Q1 FY 2025 net income of ¥2,415 million to ¥447 million in Q3 adds to that concern, because it shows profits can vary a lot within a single year.

Margins at 11.5% on ¥33.6b sales

  • Supporters with a more bullish tilt argue that this higher margin and the description of earnings as high quality strengthen the idea that the recent profit improvement is grounded in the core business, and the quarterly pattern backs that up even with some bumps.
    • Over the last year, net income of ¥3,851 million on ¥33.6b of sales is ahead of the ¥2,791 million earned on ¥29.6b a year earlier, which lines up with the higher margin.
    • Even though Q2 FY 2025 showed a net loss of ¥77 million on ¥3.2b revenue, the swing back to ¥447 million profit on ¥5.8b revenue in Q3 shows the margin profile can recover within the year.
Curious how this profit picture feeds into the more optimistic story some investors tell? Take a look at the 🐂 Teikoku Sen-i Bull Case once you have the headline numbers in mind.

P/E premium and DCF gap at ¥10,221

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 21.4x versus 14.3x for the Japan Machinery industry and 16.9x for peers, while a DCF fair value of ¥10,221.14 compared with the current ¥3,225 price and a 1.71% dividend yield creates a wide gap between this earnings multiple and that model based estimate plus income stream.

    • On one hand, investors paying 21.4x trailing earnings are valuing Teikoku Sen i more highly than the 14.3x industry and 16.9x peer averages, which can be hard to reconcile with the multi year earnings decline.
    • On the other hand, the DCF fair value of ¥10,221.14 is a little more than 3x the current ¥3,225 price, and that large model gap plus a 1.71% yield can appeal to investors who give more weight to the recent 38% earnings growth and improved 11.5% margin.
